markdown:
## Analysis is Not Fake


1. I know what you're thinking, sometimes analysis** feels fake**.
2. So **what's the point**?
3. The best answer I can give you is that analysis is really about **_seeing through things. _**

> If you can’t analyse, you’re at the mercy of people who can.

![Analysis is more than this](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ef0e59c83a3f7a6cd2a913ebdb19edea8e581ca9-1276x1126.png)

## Because Language is Never Neutral


1. Every text you read tries to **shape how you see the world**.
2. Think about:
  1. An **advert **that says “New look. Same great taste.”
  2. A **politician** who says “We're protecting families” instead of “cutting immigration.”
  3. A **breakup text **that says “I just need time to work on myself.”
3. All of these are **carefully worded** to make you feel something **specific**.
4. Language has the power to make** lies sound reasonable** and **strong emotions sound weak**. 
5. And most of the time, people **don’t even notice it happening**.

![Analysis trains you to read between the lines](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/232663c4768f614d6d713be6e4d3b3e4c7077dd7-1630x404.png)

## Purpose: Writers Always Write for a Reason


<definition term="Purpose">What the writer wants you to think, feel, or do.</definition>

1. This is the **why** behind the text and you **must **uncover it.
2. To start, understand most texts have **more than one purpose**. 
3. It helps to break this down into:
  1. **Primary purpose**: the writer’s **main** goal. What they **most want** the reader to **do**, **think**, or **feel**.
  2. **Secondary purpose**: an **additional** goal that **supports** or **disguises** the real intent.

<callout type="example">* Writer's might write to: 
  * Convince you that** mining companies** are **destroying the planet**
  * Express the **beauty of pigeons**
  * Prove that **pizza is the best food**</callout>

<callout type="tip">* A writer’s purpose might be **obvious **(e.g. "advertise this product") or more **subtle** ("make the reader feel guilty"). 
* Good analysis always thinks about the **effect on the reader**.</callout>

## Language: How Purpose Is Conveyed


1. **Words **are the **only tools** a writer has. 
2. So they have to be chosen **_very_** carefully.
3. Writers use:
  1. **Literary techniques** (metaphor, alliteration, juxtaposition...)
  2. **Structural features** (contrast, repetition, tone shifts...)
  3. **Specific choices** (verbs, adjectives, punctuation...)

## Effect: Why Language Makes You Feel Something


1. Language always does something to the reader. It might:
  1. **Make an idea clearer** (intellectual effect)
  2. **Trigger an emotion** (emotional effect)

<callout type="example">* If a breakup text says, _"I just need time to work on myself"_, the **language softens the blow** and **hides the full truth**. 
* It creates the effect of making the speaker sound **reasonable**, and **avoids conflict**.</callout>

## Context: Writer's Respond To The World Around Them


1. Why is your favorite songwriter writing about the **end of a relationship**? Probably because they **just ended one**. 
2. Why does this ad keep referring to the **World Cup**? Probably because it's **coming up soon**.
3. Understanding context is a shortcut to getting to the **writer's why**. 

<callout type="hint">**Historical events**, **cultural values**, and **personal experiences **are all ways you can think about context.</callout>

## You're Already Doing It


1. When someone says “**no offence but**” you **already know it's going to be offensive. **
2. When a friend says “I’m fine” with a **weird tone**, you don’t believe it.
3. This is analysis **in real life**. You’re **reading between the lines**. 
4. English class just gives you the** tools** and** language** to do that in a **structured** way.

<callout type="tip">* Analysis teaches you to:
  * **Spot manipulation** in ads, speeches, media, news
  * Understand how **identity**, **power**, and **culture **shape the way texts are written and read</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">* At first glance, the ad seems designed to **inspire** you. 
* But Nike is a **business **whose goals will always be to **maximize** **profit.**
* Therefore, while its **secondary purpose** is to inspire, its **primary purpose** is to **sell**. 
* The **real** goal is to make you feel **motivated enough** to **buy new gear**.
* The **emotional message** supports the **financial goal**.

![Nike's "Find your greatness" ad](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3752244d26c15130e7ccdc15fa5ace498d2a7095-1218x590.png)</callout>
